source.title: Demo reviewed tearing chest back pain case
source.citation: Local reviewed educational case file for Diagnostician demo content.
source.raw_text: De-identified educational case source assembled and reviewed for demo validation.

case.title: Abrupt tearing chest pain radiating to the back
case.chief_complaint: Sudden severe chest and back pain.
teaching_points: Abrupt maximal pain radiating to the back with pulse or pressure asymmetry requires urgent vascular imaging.
A normal initial troponin does not exclude life-threatening non-coronary causes of chest pain.
Blood pressure control and anatomy determine management urgency.

facts.label: Opening concern
facts.value: The patient reports sudden, severe chest pain radiating between the shoulder blades.

provenance.quote: Sudden severe chest pain radiating to the back.

facts.label: Pain onset
facts.value: The pain reached maximal intensity within seconds while he was lifting a box.

provenance.quote: Maximal intensity within seconds while lifting a box.

facts.label: Risk history
facts.value: He has long-standing hypertension and stopped taking his blood pressure medication last month.

provenance.quote: Long-standing hypertension; stopped blood pressure medication last month.

facts.label: Associated symptoms
facts.value: He feels sweaty and anxious but denies cough, fever, or pleuritic pain.

provenance.quote: Diaphoresis and anxiety; no cough, fever, or pleuritic pain.

facts.label: Triage vitals
facts.value: Blood pressure is 196/104 mmHg in the right arm and 168/92 mmHg in the left arm; heart rate is 108/min.

provenance.quote: BP 196/104 right arm, 168/92 left arm; HR 108.

facts.label: Vascular exam
facts.value: The left radial pulse is slightly diminished compared with the right; neurologic exam is nonfocal.

provenance.quote: Left radial pulse diminished compared with right; nonfocal neuro exam.

facts.label: Troponin
facts.value: Initial high-sensitivity troponin is not elevated.

facts.label: CBC and chemistry
facts.value: CBC and basic chemistry do not show an alternate explanation for the acute pain.

facts.label: Chest radiograph
facts.value: Chest radiograph shows a widened mediastinal contour without pneumothorax.

facts.label: CTA chest
facts.value: CTA shows an intimal flap beginning distal to the left subclavian artery and extending through the descending thoracic aorta.

provenance.quote: Intimal flap distal to left subclavian extending through descending thoracic aorta.

facts.label: Dangerous differential
facts.value: A catastrophic vascular cause of chest pain should be considered with abrupt onset and asymmetric vascular findings.

provenance.quote: Abrupt pain, pulse deficit, blood pressure asymmetry, and CTA anatomy indicate vascular emergency.

facts.label: Hint 1
facts.value: Do not anchor on coronary disease; the asymmetric arm findings and pain quality change the priority.

provenance.quote: Pulse and pressure asymmetry are the key clues.

facts.label: Final diagnosis
facts.value: Aortic dissection supported by abrupt tearing chest and back pain, hypertension, arm pressure difference, pulse asymmetry, and CTA intimal flap.
